sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
So this project now is basically all to, to, to tell the story what happened during the Holocaust, before and after.

I was-- after I retired-- I was in retirement-- I realized that if I would tell the story-- if somebody would take me in court and, and-- to prove that my parents were, were killed and my two brothers, I would have no proof at all.
And their story is, is accepted worldwide.
And they, they are prove-- they are trying to prove that why the-- why the-- why they're denying the Holocaust.
They're not denying actually the Holocaust itself.
They say the Holocaust took place.
People-- Jewish people have been killed.
The second thing these historians were saying-- that, technically, it's impossible also to kill.
Because they are researching in Auschwitz and all these concentration camps.
And they're saying that the, the, the gas over there, the crematoriums, were meant not to kill Jews.
They were meant to, to delouse them, delouse the people.
The inmates who were, were getting the, the, the typhus sickness because they were-- they had the louse.

So now to kill all these insects, they used to put in there the, the dresses, the, the, the-- of the-- of the inmates and, and to ssst-- to sanitize them, you know, to, to kill all these insects and bugs and the louse.
And then all kind of debate, all kind of technical things that it's impossible.
And there is a professor, Deborah--
In other words-- in other words, I, I was impressed that this thing is-- why is this thing happening?
So I was very much hurt.
And I was thinking of the reason-- how this could be?

So I came to a conclusion that it could be only because most of the people who believe that six million Jews got killed by the Nazis, not those 22% who says that it's impossible, that they don't believe it.
But according to the last survey, the 78% who do believe, they-- the same people who believe that this happened, they are-- what they are saying is, yes, we believe.
But we cannot understand.
How could it be done?
So what does it-- what do you mean, it cannot be so?
So I was thinking.
And I was-- and I went to Jerusalem in the-- all  over the Yad Vashem.
And I collected evidence, historical evidence about, about the Judenrat.
I, I took-- I have downstairs books from the-- from historians, which they researched the history of the Judenrat during the Holocaust.

In the ghettos, these Jewish councils who were running these ghettos-- after the, the, the-- after-- in the beginning of the war, they-- Einsatzgruppen, all those special groups who were supposed to take care and, and-- of the-- of the Jews in, in the small towns.
They got an order from, from one of the masterminders of the Holocaust, Heydrich and Eichmann, to concentrate the Jewish from the small towns into a large ghetto.
Oh, the, the smallest was-- there was in Warsaw, 500,000 in the ghetto; in Lodz, 300,000-- Lodz Ghettos.

And these Jewish councils knew that the Germans would not kill them because they would have to use the labor the young-- of the young people, which is a very, very very very helpful for the-- for the German Army.
They needed some kind of work for them to do.
Like, like the, the movie, the Schindler, he, he figured out that by using this Jewish labor, he will make profit, make money for himself.
At the same time, they, they, they, they, they, they survived the war.
But he didn't do it for, for the purpose to, to help them to survive the war.
The same thing in the ghettos.
They didn't do it at that time-- the, the Wehrmacht-- to help the Jews.
But they needed their labor and their work and their-- and their specialty.
They were mechanics.
They were tailors.
They were nurses, doctors.
All those work they could use during the war.
And that's why the Jewish Council hoping because by this way, they will-- they will last and survive the-- and, and, and, and last-- in the war was-- they will survive.
But in the meantime, they got calls from-- to, to send in more and more people, old people and children and women who are not working.
And they knew that these people which they are sending away are not sending for resettlement, but to be sent to concentration camps to get killed.

And this was the, the hidden-- the hidden factor, the hidden element which helped the Nazis to do it smoothly and quickly and, and to kill the six million Jews with, with-- by, by, by complying with the order of the Gestapo to send these people without any-- without any-- suspicion that they're going to go get killed.
Yes.
The project--
The project--
Yes.
The project is basically based on this idea of the Judenrat.

I did a-- I did a cable-- on cable television, on Staten Island, a program-- a one-hour program, in which-- in which participated a panel of two rabbis-- three rabbis, and the President of the Second Generation, Mrs.
Shelly Schore.

And we debated the program, which they-- which they show-- they, they show on the television, which was including a, a monologue by myself, and a skit named which was a skit-- a, a script of a-- of a-- of a show which took place in the ghetto in Wilno during the Holocaust.
A skit which I wrote.
It's not a fictional.
It's based on a true story, which I took it from, from somebody who was in this ghetto.
And he wrote about it.
It was my writing, yes.
But I based the whole skit on a true story, which was-- which was written by-- the story was written by Chaim Lazar, who was a known Jewish Holocaust survivor and a partisan in the ghettos.
And he was active in this-- in this project of the Holocaust.

So then I wrote also another-- I wrote a-- I, I wrote by myself a, a book, which is called This is a book which Jews, Haggadah, they call during the Passover-- in Passover Seder, Seder dinner, in which they remember the exodus of Jewish slaves from Egypt.
And during that evening, the child is asking four questions their parents.
Why do we have this dinner?
Why do we have matzoh during Passover?
What does this mean?
What  is this remembering us?
And all these questions are included in four questions.

So I wrote such a book, which is called in which this child, in addition to the four questions, he's asking the fifth question-- 'How could it happen?' which is not what this project of, of-- which is also a good project of Spielberg, basically, to-- the goal was what happened?
My project is to answer the question, how could it happen?
How could  it be done?
Why did God let it happen?
Why did the Jews let themselves killed like sheep unto the slaughter?
All these unanswered questions which nobody, so far, has-- was dealing with, with these problems-- or these questions.
And, and that's why people cannot understand up to even, so far, nobody can understand and comprehend this mind-boggling number of six million Jews got killed.
Also, I wrote--
--my, my my life story, my autobiography.
Yes, I wrote it.
And I-- the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC, wrote me-- wrote me a letter.
They were ready to accept my book after they had read it.
They read the story.
And anybody who wants to read my-- the story-- my story, they call it It's in the library of the fifth floor in the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC.
And there is my whole life story written down.
Yes.
It was a time when my wife-- we concluded-- and she finished the first chemotherapy of two years.
After two years, she was in remission.
And we're celebrating this, this event.
We took this trip for Europe.
And we stopped in Israel.
We stopped in Budapest, where I was walking around the places where I used to be young and before the war and during the war.
And from there, we took a trip to Cracow.
In Cracow, we visited the, the synagogue over there.
And we took-- we hired a cab.
And he took us with his cab straight to Auschwitz Concentration Camp.
And we visited the whole day in, in Auschwitz.
We took pictures.
And from there, this cabbie put us to a train station in Katowice.
We took a train from Katowice to Prague.
In Prague, we took a cabbie.
And he took us to Karlsbad.
It was a famous health spa.
My wife, she needs this health spa.
We were staying there for about 10 days, about 2 weeks.
And once my wife, when she saw I was going to sleep, she went and she picked up the phone without my knowledge.
And she made-- making-- she contacted the operator in, in, in, in, in the city of Nachod.
And somebody was helping her to, to, to, translate from English to Czech.
And she said to this operator, please, we are in Karlsbad.
We are looking for somebody by the name, Jirka Kuchar.
So she looked in the-- in the book.
And she got somebody who had entered his name.
And he was on the phone telling her, I'm Jirka Kuchar.
And she said, but do you know this and this?
He said, no, this is-- you probably want-- you want another Jirka.
There's another Jirka Kuchar.
His father was Jewish.
You're probably looking to this Jirka Kuchar.
I know him.
I'm going to tell him to get in touch with you, give you my phone number.
And he will call you up.
So in the-- it was in the morning, around-- I don't remember exactly.
There was a phone call.
And I was wondering, who was calling us at this time?
Who knows we are here?
So my wife picks up the phone.
And she says, they want you on the phone.
And I pick up the phone and says, yes?
And he says in Czech, if I'm Emil Jacoby.
I says, yeah-- Jacobovitz?
I says, yes.
And he says, no, I'm your son.
What do you mean, you're my son?
[LAUGHS] So this is the way-- make a story short, I called him over to the hotel.
He came with his wife.
And he had a boy.
And he came from Nachod to Karlsbad to see us.
And we met.
And there, we give him my, my address and phone number, and promised to get-- we promised to, to be in touch-- to keep in touch.
So when we got home, our daughter told them the-- my wife told our daughter the story.
Both daughters were here.
And they, they were so happy that we found a brother for them.
So they said, we want to see our brother.
And we invited this son to come here to visit us.
And he stayed with us for about a month.
And we had fun.
And we enjoyed.
And then-- now we are in touch.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] I have a message.
I don't know if I will succeed in, in, in completing.
My message is just-- is only an idea.

And as long as this idea has not materialized into a-- something concrete which people can learn from this something, then I, I cannot-- anything would be-- to, to, to show off and, and tell that I'm somebody who has a message.
But my, my purpose is to make this work.

And I hope that I will succeed in making this fifth question and to put it into some kind of a forum of a-- of a show that will-- could be-- could be shown either on the-- on the television, or, or by other media, so that people will be willing to see this, this story, will have this patience to sit there for half an hour or for an hour and learn-- and something to have out of it.

Because, because if we don't-- if we don't work on this-- on my project and people will keep-- will keep having doubts and not having faith in this-- in this-- in this story that six million Jews got killed, then more and more people will believe in the books and those denials of the Holocaust.
Because there will-- because in the next generation, it's not that-- you can-- you can show them pictures about what happened, museums, and all these stories which I'm saying.
I cannot prove anything that my parents got killed and my brothers got killed.
So that no-- many people, Holocaust survivors cannot prove it.
So they will stop believing that it is true.
So this can lead to something very, very difficult.
They're very terrible for the Jewish people, that they were coming and making a hoax out of the six million Jews killed.
And they will believe that it is a hoax, only because they will not understand-- the next generation-- how it could be done.
We have to stick to this problem that not only we have to know what happened, we have also to understand how it could be done.
Otherwise, six million-- you  will believe that there was a Holocaust.
Jews got killed.
But they will not believe six million Jews got killed.
This will be a very bad situation for the Jewish people.
Because we are only the, the last Mohicans now.
We are getting-- dying off, this generation.
Another year-- you wouldn't find, in a couple of years, another Holocaust survivor.
So who is going to tell the story?
Don't you think that the, the fact that you're doing this testimony--
Yes.
Well, you are putting my story what happened, right?
But--
Yes, but this is not enough because they want to see something attractive.

They want to see this story involved in some kind of entertainment, like, like, if Spielberg would show only what happened in Cracow with this-- with this-- with this how Jews were killed and how they suffered, they wouldn't be such a big success.
He managed to put in some kind of a story, a human story, that was appealing to some people and entertaining.
And they went to see two hours and sit in the movie and watch it.
Otherwise, they wouldn't go to watch it.
Now I found somebody.
There's a girl here in Staten Island.
She's working on such a story.
If I manage to make this story, then I can-- maybe in a few years, I can produce a real story which people will see-- oh, I saw something, which I learned something.
This would be both educational and historical, based on historical facts.
And they will also get-- have fun in seeing something most interesting.
Otherwise, people will never be willing to sit and watch it on TV or in a movie-- my story.
Because it's just a, a story.

Yes, I would like to thank-- to say-- to express my deep appreciation and gratitude to all of you who are working with these Holocaust survivors in bringing their story so that never can be forgotten what happened.
Even if you are not dealing specifically on the other side of the-- of the coin, which is how it could happen.
But this is also a very noble goal, what you are doing.
And Mr.

Steven Spielberg himself, who, who donated all this money-- this financing for all these projects, I myself give a token of appreciation by sending him my painted in, in, in oil color, his portrait, for which he sent me a nice letter thanking me for my gift to him.
This is a picture of my father, Mendel Jacobovitz, Jacobovitz.
The picture was taken in Chicago, Illinois, when he came from America to Czechoslovakia.
And he put this picture on his passport-- American passport.
That's about-- after World War I.
It could be approximately 1919 or 1920.
On this picture, we see myself, Emil Jacoby.
At that time, I was called Emil Jacobovitz, and Betty, at that time called Bertina Bercu.
It was in the-- 1951, when we-- at the time that we were engaged.
It was in, in Harduf.
In Israel.
This picture was taken in April the 3rd, 1952, during our wedding ceremony in Ra'anana, Israel.
On this picture is myself, Emil Jacoby, and Betty Bercu.
This picture was taken approximately in 1960 in Israel after was-- Rachel and Nahama were born.
And we see on this picture myself, Emil Jacoby, and my wife, Betty Jacoby, and the parents of my wife.
That was the-- was the father-in-law, Bercu-- Julius-- and the mother-in-law, Esther Bercu.
This picture was taken at the B'nai Jeshuron Synagogue in Staten Island in October, 1992, during the bat mitzvah party of our granddaughter, Sheera Borenstein.

On this picture, we see myself, my wife, on the other side, Betty, our, our son-in-law, Gabe Borenstein, and our daughter, Rachel Borenstein, with our grandchildren, with our daughter-in-law, Nahama Cohen.
Daughter-in-law Nahama.
Daughter.
I mean, daughter, Nahama.
And our grandchildren, [?
Estee ?] Borenstein and Yossi Borenstein.
There's [?
Maya ?] Cohen and Sheera Borenstein.
This picture was also taken during the bat mitzvah in 1992, during the bat mitzvah of Sheera Borenstein.
And you have all the six grandchildren on this picture.
From right to left, there is [?
Maya ?] Cohen.
There is [?
Estee ?] Borenstein, Yossi Borenstein, [?
Liat ?] Cohen.
And there's Oren Cohen and [?
Estee ?] Borenstein.
On this picture, we are together, me and my wife, Betty, visiting Auschwitz in 1987, Auschwitz Concentration Camp.
The, the, the place is Birkenau, actually.
Next to Auschwitz, there's another camp called Birkenau.
This picture was taken in 1988, in Manhattan, New York, when our-- when my son, Jirka Kuchar, came to visit us from Czechoslovakia.
